frontmatter.title: Consistent subsets of an event structure

mainmatter.p: A subset x \subseteq E of an event structure is said to be consistent when any finite subset of x lies in \mathsf { Con } _{ E }.

link.title: On the expressivity of symmetry in event structures

mainmatter.p: An event structure is defined to be triple { \mathopen {} \left ( E, \mathsf { Con } _{ E } , \leq _E \right ) \mathclose {}} where E is a partial order of events partially ordered by the causal dependency relation \leq _E, and \mathsf { Con } _{ E } \subseteq \mathcal {P} _{ \mathit {fin} } { \mathopen {} \left ( E \right ) \mathclose {}} specifies when a finite subset of E is consistent, satisfying the following laws:

ol.li: every downset of E is finite;
any subset of a consistent subset is consistent;
for any consistent subset x \in \mathsf { Con } _{ E } and e \leq e' with e' \in x, we have x \cup { \mathopen {} \left \{ e \right \} \mathclose {}} \in \mathsf { Con } _{ E }.

mainmatter.p: The definition of event structure only speaks of finite consistent subsets; these are extended to arbitrary subsets as follows.

link.title: On the expressivity of symmetry in event structures

mainmatter.p: A configuration of an event structure E is defined to be a consistent, downward-closed subset of E. We will write \mathcal {C} _{ E } for the set of all configurations of E and \mathcal {C} _{ E }^{ \mathit {fin} } for the set of finite configurations of E.

link.title: On the expressivity of symmetry in event structures

mainmatter.p: A rigid morphism of event structures { E } \xrightarrow {{ f }}{ E' } is a monotone function on events that preserves finite configurations and is locally injective in the sense that for any finite configuration x \in \mathcal {C} _{ E }^{ \mathit {fin} } and events e_1,e_2 \in x such that fe_1 = fe_2, we have e_1=e_2.

I have not checked whether the appropriate structure identity principle holds for the (pre)category of event structures and rigid morphisms. If it does not hold, this would suggest that a new univalent definition of event structure is needed.

link.title: On the expressivity of symmetry in event structures

mainmatter.p: An event structure is defined to be triple { \mathopen {} \left ( E, \mathsf { Con } _{ E } , \leq _E \right ) \mathclose {}} where E is a partial order of events partially ordered by the causal dependency relation \leq _E, and \mathsf { Con } _{ E } \subseteq \mathcal {P} _{ \mathit {fin} } { \mathopen {} \left ( E \right ) \mathclose {}} specifies when a finite subset of E is consistent, satisfying the following laws:

ol.li: every downset of E is finite;
any subset of a consistent subset is consistent;
for any consistent subset x \in \mathsf { Con } _{ E } and e \leq e' with e' \in x, we have x \cup { \mathopen {} \left \{ e \right \} \mathclose {}} \in \mathsf { Con } _{ E }.

link.title: On the expressivity of symmetry in event structures

mainmatter.p: A configuration of an event structure E is defined to be a consistent, downward-closed subset of E. We will write \mathcal {C} _{ E } for the set of all configurations of E and \mathcal {C} _{ E }^{ \mathit {fin} } for the set of finite configurations of E.
